static.description: A polyglot microservices platform for food delivery, built with 5 independent services (API Gateway, Order, Inventory, Driver, Notification) across Kotlin, Java, Python, Go, and TypeScript stacks, communicating via RabbitMQ events and fronted by a JWT-authenticated API Gateway.
domain_concepts: OrderStatus: Enum representing the lifecycle states of an order: PENDING → ACCEPTED → PREPARING → READY_FOR_PICKUP → IN_TRANSIT → DELIVERED, with CANCELLED and REFUNDED as terminal/side states.
OrderItem: An embeddable JPA entity representing a line item within an order, containing menu item ID, name, quantity, and unit price.
MenuItem: A SQLAlchemy model representing a restaurant menu item with stock_quantity (-1 means unlimited) and availability tracking.
Driver: A MongoDB document representing a delivery driver with name, phone, vehicle type, availability status, and GPS location.
GeoPoint: A struct/document embedded in Driver storing latitude and longitude coordinates for real-time location tracking.
JwtAuthFilter: A Spring Cloud Gateway global filter that validates JWT tokens and forwards the verified customer ID as an X-Customer-Id header to downstream services.
acquire_lock: A Redis-based distributed lock function in the Inventory Service that prevents concurrent stock overselling by acquiring an exclusive lock on a menu item.
order.events: The RabbitMQ exchange name used for publishing order lifecycle events that the Notification Service consumes.
stripePaymentIntentId: The Stripe PaymentIntent ID (pi_xxxx) stored on an Order, used for manual capture and refund operations.
Restaurant: A SQLAlchemy model representing a restaurant with name, address, cuisine type, rating, and associated menu items.
isValidTransition: A static method on OrderStatus that enforces the allowed state machine transitions for order lifecycle.

dynamic.architecture_summary: The system follows a microservices architecture with an API Gateway (Kotlin/Spring Cloud Gateway) performing JWT authentication and routing to downstream services: Order Service (Java/Spring Boot + PostgreSQL + Stripe + RabbitMQ), Inventory Service (Python/FastAPI + PostgreSQL + Redis), Driver Service (Go + MongoDB), and Notification Service (Node.js/TypeScript consuming RabbitMQ events via Twilio/SendGrid). A React/Vite frontend communicates through the gateway. Services communicate asynchronously via RabbitMQ order lifecycle events.

key_components.name: API Gateway
key_components.responsibility: Routes requests to downstream services and enforces JWT authentication via a global filter

key_components.name: JWT Auth Filter
key_components.responsibility: Validates JWT tokens and injects X-Customer-Id header for downstream services

key_components.name: Order Service
key_components.responsibility: Manages order CRUD, lifecycle state transitions, Stripe payment integration, and publishes events to RabbitMQ

key_components.name: Order Controller
key_components.responsibility: REST API for creating, listing, updating status, and cancelling orders

key_components.name: Order Service Logic
key_components.responsibility: Business logic for order creation with payment, status transitions with validation, cancellation, and event publishing

key_components.name: Order Model
key_components.responsibility: JPA entity representing an order with items, status, and Stripe payment intent

key_components.name: OrderStatus
key_components.responsibility: Defines valid order lifecycle states and validates state transitions

key_components.name: Stripe Payment Gateway
key_components.responsibility: Live Stripe SDK integration for PaymentIntent creation and refunds

key_components.name: Inventory Service
key_components.responsibility: FastAPI app for restaurant/menu queries and stock reservation with Redis distributed locks

key_components.name: Inventory Models
key_components.responsibility: SQLAlchemy models for Restaurant and MenuItem with stock management semantics

key_components.name: Driver Service
key_components.responsibility: Go HTTP service for driver CRUD, location tracking, and availability management backed by MongoDB

key_components.name: Notification Service
key_components.responsibility: Consumes RabbitMQ order events and sends SMS (Twilio) and email (SendGrid) notifications with idempotency

key_components.name: Frontend App
key_components.responsibility: React SPA with role-based views for customers, restaurants, drivers, and admins

key_components.name: Auth Context
key_components.responsibility: React context for JWT token management and customer ID extraction

key_components.name: API Client
key_components.responsibility: Axios instance configured with base URL and automatic JWT token injection

agent_safe_operations: Adding new API endpoints to existing services
Adding new unit tests to any service
Adding new frontend API client functions
Adding new TypeScript types in frontend/src/types/
Adding new Go handler methods in driver-service
Adding new order status transition logic in OrderStatus.java
Adding or modifying Pydantic models in inventory-service
agent_forbidden_operations: Altering the openapi.yaml specification without coordinating all services
Changing JWT secret or authentication mechanisms
Modifying database migration strategies or dropping tables
Changing RabbitMQ exchange names or routing key patterns without updating all consumers
Modifying the agent-context.json or AGENTS.md files
potential_pitfalls: Each service uses a different language, build system, and test framework — changes must be made with the correct toolchain for each service
The order-service uses Maven (pom.xml) while the api-gateway uses Gradle (build.gradle.kts) — do not confuse build commands
The inventory-service uses async SQLAlchemy with asyncpg — all database operations must be awaited and use AsyncSession
Redis distributed locks in inventory-service have a 10-second TTL — long-running operations could cause lock expiry and race conditions
The Notification Service idempotency is in-memory (Set) — it resets on restart, so duplicate messages are possible after service restarts
The Driver Service Go code uses a DriverStore interface — all new data operations must go through this interface, not directly to MongoDB
Frontend LoginView generates dev JWTs client-side with a hardcoded secret — this must never be used in production
The docker-compose.yml is truncated in the repo — the inventory-service and remaining services may have incomplete configuration
OrderStatus.isValidTransition enforces a strict state machine — adding new statuses requires updating all transition rules
The API Gateway forwards X-Customer-Id header — downstream services trust this header implicitly, so bypassing the gateway breaks auth
The frontend hardcodes baseURL to localhost:8080 — this will fail in non-local environments
The order-service publishes to 'order.events' RabbitMQ exchange — the notification-service must bind to the same exchange name and routing pattern
